coredata.dc:title: TMPyP-bound guanosine-borate supramolecular hydrogel as smart hemoperfusion device with real-time visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitoring for selective blood lead elimination
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiang
Yan, Xin
Wang, Bin-cheng
Xue, Wei
Zhou, Chang-ren
Zhang, Yi
Liu, Zong-hua
coredata.dc:description: Blood lead poisoning is a universal and severe health problem that greatly threatens human health in various industries. Elimination of blood lead relying on chelating agents and combination with hemoperfusion adsorbents has achieved considerable progress, but it is still suffering from the compromised selectivity of adsorbents as well as in absence of real time monitoring during treatment. Herein, we proposed a selective blood lead adsorbent integrated with real-time visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitoring based on TMPyP-bound guanosine-borate (GB) supramolecular hydrogel as potential smart hemoperfusion device. The GB hydrogel possessed stability in physiological environment, self-healing ability resistant to fluid shear, blood compatibility, selective adsorption of lead ions superior to conventional adsorbents, anti-fouling performance to blood components and renewability. Benefiting from binding with TMPyP and the intrinsic conductivity, GB hydrogel was endowed with the ability to qualitatively diagnose the presence of blood lead via simple color change and quantitatively reflect the amount of adsorbed lead from blood accurately through electrochemical technique. This work puts forward an integrated treatment/monitoring hemoperfusion device with high selectivity, simple fabrication and low-cost, providing a paradigm for next generation design of intelligent, monitorable theranostic hemopurification system, which is also an extensible platform for the other research fields such as environmental monitoring and remediation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blood lead elimination
Supramolecular hydrogel
Hemoperfusion
Flexible bi-modal sensor
Adsorbents

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GB hydrogel before and after lead ions adsorption
Lead ions adsorption capacity of GB hydrogel
Selective adsorption ability and renewability of GB hydrogel
Competitive adsorption with different metal ions
Blood lead adsorption with cycled treatment
Qualitative visualization of lead ions adsorption by color change
Quantitative monitoring of lead ions adsorption through electrochemical signals
Evaluation of the bi-modal monitoring system for blood lead adsorption
Characterization of GB hydrogel after lead ions adsorption
Lead ions adsorption property of GB hydrogel
Selective adsorption ability and renewability of GB hydrogel
Qualitative visualization and electrochemical quantification of lead ions adsorption
Evaluation of the bi-modal monitoring system during blood lead adsorption

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GB hydrogel before and after lead ions treatment: (a) Appearance change of GB hydrogel after lead ions treatments. (b) SEM image and pore area distribution of GB hydrogel before and after lead ions treatment. (c) Elastic modulus of GB hydrogel treated with different concentrations of lead ions. (d) Circular dichroism of GB hydrogel before and after lead ions treatment.

Lead ions adsorption plots of GB hydrogel: (a) Lead ions adsorption kinetic of GB hydrogel over the time. (b) Fitting curve of adsorption kinetic to pseudo-second-order kinetic model. (c) Lead ions adsorption isotherm of GB hydrogel. (d) Fitting curve to Freundlich isothermal adsorption model. (e) Plasma protein adsorption of GB hydrogel compared with activated charcoal. (f) RBCs adsorption of GB hydrogel compared with activated charcoal. (g) Metal ions competitive adsorption into GB hydrogel. (h) Adsorption-regeneration cycles of GB hydrogel to Pb2+. **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001 analyzed using the ANOVA test.

Blood lead adsorption: (a) Time-dependent blood lead removal using GB hydrogel with activated charcoal as a control. **p < 0.01. (b) Residual blood lead concentration measured with adsorption time. (c) Blood lead removal efficiency with treatment cycles using GB hydrogel. (d) Residual blood lead concentration measured with adsorption cycles.

(a) Rheological properties of GB hydrogel in the presence (red) or absence (blue) of TMPyP. (b) The absorption peak of TMPyP in UV–Vis spectra right shifted after binding to the GB hydrogel. (c) The fluorescence quenching in the TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel treated with different concentrations of Pb2+ solution. (d) The color of TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel changed from brown to green after incubation with different concentrations of Pb2+ solution. (e) Cyclic voltammograms that change with the lead ion adsorption quantity acquired from TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel. (f) Linear fitting curve between lead ions adsorption amount and anodic peak current at 0.4 V. (g) Cyclic voltammograms that change with the blood lead quantity acquired from GB hydrogel. The inset represents the color change of GB hydrogel after incubation with lead poisoned human blood. (h) Linear fitting curve between lead ions adsorption amount and anodic peak current at 0.4 V. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic of GB hydrogel-based smart hemoperfusion device for selective blood lead elimination with qualitative visualized color indicator and quantitative electrochemical monitoring.

head.ce:title: TMPyP-bound guanosine-borate supramolecular hydrogel as smart hemoperfusion device with real-time visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitoring for selective blood lead elimination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering and Technological Research Center for Drug Carrier Development, Department of Biomedical Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering and Technological Research Center for Drug Carrier Development

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Engineering and Technological Research Center for Drug Carrier Development, Department of Biomedical Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blood lead poisoning is a universal and severe health problem that greatly threatens human health in various industries. Elimination of blood lead relying on chelating agents and combination with hemoperfusion adsorbents has achieved considerable progress, but it is still suffering from the compromised selectivity of adsorbents as well as in absence of real time monitoring during treatment. Herein, we proposed a selective blood lead adsorbent integrated with real-time visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitoring based on TMPyP-bound guanosine-borate (GB) supramolecular hydrogel as potential smart hemoperfusion device. The GB hydrogel possessed stability in physiological environment, self-healing ability resistant to fluid shear, blood compatibility, selective adsorption of lead ions superior to conventional adsorbents, anti-fouling performance to blood components and renewability. Benefiting from binding with TMPyP and the intrinsic conductivity, GB hydrogel was endowed with the ability to qualitatively diagnose the presence of blood lead via simple color change and quantitatively reflect the amount of adsorbed lead from blood accurately through electrochemical technique. This work puts forward an integrated treatment/monitoring hemoperfusion device with high selectivity, simple fabrication and low-cost, providing a paradigm for next generation design of intelligent, monitorable theranostic hemopurification system, which is also an extensible platform for the other research fields such as environmental monitoring and remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal poisoning has greatly threatened human health by causing toxic effects and its related disease is a remarkable problem drawing attention over the world (Yang et al., 2018). Considered as a systemic toxicant even at lower levels of exposure, lead ion (Pb2+) has been linked to a variety of health problems, including cardiovascular disease (Lanphear et al., 2018), intellectually deficits (Lanphear et al., 2005) and cancer (Tchounwou et al., 2012). Moreover it is reported that children are more susceptible to lead poisoning than adults (Needleman et al., 1979).

At present, the preferred method used clinically for removing blood lead is injecting chelating agents like dimercaptosuccinate (DMSA) or Disodium calcium edetate (Aaseth et al., 2018), which form complexes with lead and further excreted from the body by kidneys. Although chelating agents are effective to some extent, inevitable toxic effects make their therapeutic efficacy questionable, specifically possible brain damage in children and the loss of zinc ions (Kosnett 2010). Alternatively, hemoperfusion is another clinical treatment to extract toxicants from the blood using adsorbent materials that could decrease the burden on kidneys (Winchester 2002). Most of the adsorbents for hemoperfusion are porous materials with high specific surface area, such as the widely used activated charcoal, which cannot selectively extract toxicants from the blood components and thus result in the significant blood component loss and related a series of side effects (Weston et al., 1977). Currently, several groups have focused on the development of new types of perfusion materials (Guo et al., 2019; Xiang et al., 2017), such as binding chelating agents to magnetic particles to achieve magnetic separation and avoid the chelating agents from entering the blood circulation. Although these strategies have achieved good results, they all neglected the selective removal of lead ions, resulting in the loss of normal blood components. Hence, there is an urgent need to develop novel perfusion materials that possess selective lead ions adsorption capacity, facile fabrication and low cost, avoiding the involvement of adsorbents into the blood circulation and minimizing side effects.

Supramolecular hydrogel is a kind of colloidal network that self-assembled by low molecular mass units or polymer through specific, directional and dynamic non-covalent interactions (Appel et al., 2012; Sangeetha and Maitra 2005). Due to its ability to entrap a large amount of water, inherent biocompatibility and high loading capacity, supramolecular hydrogel is applied in a wide variety of biomedical applications, such as wound healing (Forero-Doria et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020), anti-bacterial materials (Wang et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020), drug delivery (Abebe et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020), and biosensing (Biswakarma et al., 2020). Guanosine-based supramolecular hydrogels have received immense interests for devising programmable smart biomaterials due to their unique self-assembly structure (Biswas et al., 2018; Peters et al. 2014, 2015; Plank and Davis 2016; Rotaru et al., 2017), especially the G4-quartet·M+ borate (GB) hydrogel. In virtue of cation (like K+ or Na+) coordination in the central cavity, guanosine and boric acid form macrocyclic G-quartet motifs that stack into GB wires by π-π stacking interactions, and finally lateral twining of G-wires drives sol-to-gel gelation transition. Hence, GB hydrogel can selectively extract cations from solution and maintain stable under physiological conditions. Therefore, GB-based supramolecular hydrogel has the potential as a selective hemoperfusion adsorbent for blood lead elimination superior to conventional chelating drugs or adsorbent materials.

With the tendency of medicine toward precision and intelligence, whether the perfusion materials can monitor the content of harmful substances in real time during the treatment process and thereby provide reminder of adsorbent replacement or regeneration, will be another challenge in the future. Besides, various sensing techniques based on test strips (Hossain et al., 2012), fluorescent probes (Huang et al., 2019), Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) (Springer and Homola 2012), Surface-Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) (Sivanesan et al., 2014), electrochemistry (Bryan et al., 2013) and optical fibers (Li et al., 2014) are emerging in endlessly. Among them, the visualized color change and electrochemical techniques are typically and commonly used, for instance, the probes with ability to change color have been developed to visualize the presence of heavy metal ions (Kundu et al., 2015; Sedghi et al., 2015), and the electrochemical method based on conductive materials was also exploited to quantify the trace amount of heavy metal ions accurately (Lu et al., 2019; Thiruppathi et al., 2017). Therefore, integrating sensor-based diagnosis techniques into the hemopurification module and thereby constructing an intelligent hemoperfusion device to achieve real-time, multi-modal monitorable and selective blood lead removal, will pave a new way for the future precise and intelligent medical treatment.

In this work, we developed a smart hemoperfusion material for selective blood lead elimination with real-time visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitoring based on guanosine-borate supramolecular hydrogel (GB hydrogel), which was easily prepared through one-step self-assembly of guanosine (G), boric acid (B(OH)3) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) in aqueous solution. The prepared GB hydrogel was endowed with superior features including (1) stability in physiological environment and resistance to fluid shear stress benefiting from self-healing ability; (2) good blood compatibility; (3) anti-fouling performance to blood components; (4) highly-selective lead ions adsorbability; (4) renewability. TMPyP, an ultra-highly sensitive spectrophotometric reagent that senses lead ions, is bound into the GB hydrogel through π-π stacking with G-quadruplex, endowing the hydrogel with additional ability to qualitatively visualize lead ions adsorption by color change. Furthermore, the high ionic conductivity of GB hydrogel makes it well suitable for flexible electrochemical sensor (Zhong et al., 2018). For this reason, we utilized the GB hydrogel to fabricate a flexible electrode to quantify lead ions through electrochemical signals. By integrating multi-modal sensing techniques into hemopurification treatment, we have proposed a real-time, visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitorable hydrogel-based device with selective adsorption capacity, simple fabrication and low-cost, exhibiting advanced overall performance over other techniques published and commercially available to eliminate/detect blood lead (Table S1). Our strategy is also an extensible platform not only applying to other heavy metal except lead, but also suitable for environmental monitoring and remediation.

Guanosine (G), boric acid (B(OH)3) and lead nitrate (Pb (NO3)2) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Pb2+ standard solution (100 mg/L, 5% HNO3) and activated charcoal were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). α, β, γ, δ-Tetrakis (1-methylpyridinium-4-yl) porphyrin p-Toluenesulfonate (TMPyP) was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). All other solvents supplied by Guangzhou Chemical Reagent (China) were analytical grade and used as received. Human Blood was collected from healthy volunteers and stored in sodium citrate tubes with a blood/anticoagulant ratio of 9:1. The experiments using human blood were approved by the Laboratory Ethics Committee of Jinan University.

GB hydrogel was prepared according to our previous report (Wu et al., 2020a). In a typical preparation of 5 wt% GB hydrogel, 0.566 g of guanosine (G) and 0.056 g of B(OH)3 were mixed in 10 mL distilled water in a flask and allowed to shake on a vortex mixer for 5 min. The mixture was then heated in a 95 °C oil bath followed by adding 2.5 mL NaOH solution (16 mg/mL) under magnetic stirring. Until the turbid solution become clear, it was cooled to room temperature to obtain the GB hydrogel. Since the sodium ions in the prepared GB hydrogel were not saturated, the hydrogel was incubated in saline for 4 h to reach the adsorption saturation of sodium ions, thereby stabilizing the hydrogel. The mass-volume ratios of GB hydrogel mentioned in this paper are ratio of dry hydrogel weight to solution volume.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GB hydrogel before and after lead ions adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: To observe the changes of the GB hydrogel before and after lead ions adsorption, two hydrogel pieces were immersed in Pb2+ (2 mg/mL) containing saline solution and saline control for 4 h respectively and the surface appearance were recorded by photographs. The microstructure morphology of GB hydrogel with or without lead ions treatment was characterized using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Ultra 55, Zeiss) after freeze-drying. Image J was used to analyze SEM graphs to obtain the pore area distribution. Rheological tests were used to further demonstrate the alteration of the mechanical properties of hydrogel before and after lead ions adsorption by recording the elasticity modulus. GB hydrogel was treated with different concentration of Pb2+ solution (0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 mg/mL, in saline) and then were tested in single-frequency time sweep mode (0.1% strain, 1 Hz). The circular dichroism (CD) spectra were collected using 1 wt% GB hydrogel before and after treated with 0.2 mg/mL Pb2+ solution on a circular dichroic spectrometer (V100, Chirascan). The CD measurement was performed in the wavelength range 200–300 nm, and the concentration of the hydrogel was diluted to 0.2 mg/mL (dry weight) before the test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lead ions adsorption capacity of GB hydrogel

ce:section.ce:para:
40 mg of 5 wt% GB hydrogel was added to Pb2+ solution (150 mg/L, 4 mL in saline, pH 7.0 ± 0.2) at dry hydrogel/solution ratio of 1% (w/v) in tubes. Subsequently, all the tubes were shaken in a thermostatic shaker (100 rpm) at room temperature. After removing the hydrogel at predetermined time, the Pb2+ concentration was measured on an Inductively Coupled Plasma Optical Emission Spectrometry (ICP-OES, Thermo iCAP 7000 Series). The adsorption capacity (Q, mg/g) was calculated according to following equation:
(1)
Q
=
(
C 0
−
C t
)
V
M
Where C0 and Ct are initial and final concentrations of Pb2+. V is the volume of Pb2+ solution. M represents the mass of GB hydrogel.

Different concentrations of Pb2+ solution from 5 to 800 mg/L was prepared by dissolving lead nitrate with saline, and the pH value was adjusted to 7.0 ± 0.2. 40 mg of 5 wt% hydrogel was mixed with 4 mL Pb2+ solutions at different concentrations in tubes. All groups were placed in a thermostatic shaker (100 rpm) at 37 °C for 4 h to ensure that the hydrogel reached adsorption equilibrium. After removing the hydrogel, the residual Pb2+ concentration in tubes was measured by ICP-OES, and the equilibrium adsorption capacity (Q, mg/g) was also calculated according to equation (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective adsorption ability and renewability of GB hydrogel

ce:section.ce:para: Citrate-anticoagulated human blood was centrifuged at 1000×g for 5 min to separate the plasma and red blood cells (RBCs) for further use. 5 wt% of GB hydrogel was mixed with the plasma at different dry hydrogel/plasma ratios, the same mass of activated charcoal was introduced as the control. After shaking at 37 °C for 2 h and removing the adsorbents, the concentration of the remaining plasma protein was measured using BCA Protein Assay Kit (Thermo).

RBCs suspensions (16 v/v% in saline) were mixed with the hydrogel and activated charcoal at 37 °C for 2 h in a shaker. The hydrogel and activated charcoal were then separated from the suspensions and immersed in distilled water to extract hemoglobin from the adsorbed RBCs for detection. After centrifugation to remove the adsorbents, the OD of supernatants in two groups was measured at 540 nm (ODa), and the OD of RBCs suspension treated with water was also measured (ODr). The RBCs adsorption (%) was calculated by the following equation:
(2)
A d s o r p t i o n
( % )
=
O
D a
O
D r
×
100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Competitive adsorption with different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Competitive adsorption experiment of different metal ions was employed according to the methods previously reported (Park et al., 2016). Lead ions solution (2 mg/L) containing the same concentration of other three metal ions (K+, Na+ and Ca2+) was prepared by dissolving KCl, NaCl and CaCl2 together with Pb (NO3)2 in distilled water. GB hydrogel was incubated with the above mixed ions solution for 2 h. Concentrations of different ions adsorbed in the hydrogel were measured on an Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent 7700x) and the adsorption percentage of each ion was calculated by dividing the original concentration in solution by the resulting concentration in hydrogel.

300 mg of GB hydrogel was added to tubes containing Pb2+ solution (2 mg/L, 30 mL in saline). After incubation with lead ions solution for 4 h, the hydrogel was withdrawn from the solution and followed by quantification of the residual Pb2+ in the solution using ICP-MS. Then the above GB•Pb2+ hydrogel was suspended in 0.1 M EDTA-2Na solution (30 mL, in saline) for 4 h to desorb the Pb2+ from hydrogel, in which the Pb2+ concentration after desorption was also determined by ICP-MS. The desorbed hydrogel was used to perform another adsorption process again in the same concentration of Pb2+ solution. Such repetitive adsorption-desorption cycle was conducted for three times and the Pb2+ concentration in each step was determined by ICP-MS.

Lead ions standard solution was mixed with citrate-anticoagulated human blood for 3 h at 37 °C to prepare the lead poisoned blood (Guo et al., 2019), and the final concentrations of Pb2+ in blood were adjusted to 800 μg/L. In a time-dependent adsorption mode, 100 mg of GB Hydrogel was added to different tubes containing lead poisoned blood (2 mL) and placed in a thermostatic shaker (100 rpm) at 37 °C. Then hydrogel was removed from the blood at predetermined time and the residual Pb2+ in blood was quantified by ICP-MS. The adsorption (%) of blood lead was calculated by the equation:
(4)
A d s o r p t i o n
( % )
=
( 1
−
C t
800
)
×
100 %
Where Ct (μg/L) is the Pb2+ concentration measured at predetermined time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blood lead adsorption with cycled treatment
ce:section.ce:para:
In cycled adsorption mode, 300 mg of GB hydrogel was incubated with lead poisoned blood (6 mL, 800 μg/L Pb2+). After an adsorption interval for 1 h, the hydrogel was removed and blood sample was taken out for the measurement of the residual Pb2+ concentration by ICP-MS, followed by another adsorption cycle using new hydrogel. The entire adsorption process consisted of four cycles. The adsorption percentage was decided by:
(5)
A d s o r p t i o n
( % )
=
M a
+
M p
M
×
100 %

In this equation, Ma represents the mass of Pb2+ adsorbed in each adsorption cycle. Mp is the total mass of Pb2+ that was previously adsorbed before each adsorption cycle, and M is the initial mass of Pb2+ in blood.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative visualization of lead ions adsorption by color change
ce:section.ce:para: TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel was prepared by mixing TMPyP (150 μM) in the GB hydrogel preparation process. Rheology tests were performed to measure the mechanical properties of the hydrogel in the absence and presence of TMPyP with a single-frequency time sweep mode (0.1% strain, 1 Hz). The absorption spectra of the TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel in the range of 350–500 nm were determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Shimadzu UV-2550) in comparison to the same concentration of TMPyP aqueous solution. For visualized observation, TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel was immersed in different concentration of lead ions solution (0–10 mg/L, in saline) for 4 h incubation at 37 °C. Then the color change of the hydrogel was recorded by photographs. The fluorescence spectra (550–700 nm) of GB hydrogels treated with different concentration of Pb2+ were measured by a multimode microplate reader (BioTek Synergy H1) with an excitation wavelength at 422 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative monitoring of lead ions adsorption through electrochemical signals
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical signals were collected using an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660E, Chenhua Instrument Company, China), in which the GB hydrogel fixed indium tin oxide (ITO) glass was used as the working electrode. Pb2+ solution (2 mg/L in saline) was used as the electrolyte. The ITO glass/GB hydrogel (100 mg) was placed into an electrolytic cell containing 100 mL electrolyte. Cyclic voltammograms were collected during the lead ions adsorption process at predetermined time, and the residual lead ions concentrations in solution were also measured by ICP-MS simultaneously to calculate the adsorption capacity (Q, μg/g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the bi-modal monitoring system for blood lead adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility of the bi-modal monitoring system for blood lead adsorption, we introduced TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel into the blood lead adsorption process as the blood lead adsorbent. For qualitative visualized observation, TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel was immersed in lead poisoned blood (800 μg/L Pb2+) for 4 h incubation at 37 °C, then the color change of the hydrogel was recorded by photographs. For quantitative electrochemical monitoring, 50 mg TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel was fixed on ITO glass as the working electrode. Lead poisoned blood (50 mL, 800 μg/L Pb2+) was used as the electrolyte. Then the ITO glass/hydrogel was placed in the lead poisoned blood to conduct a time-dependent blood lead adsorption process. Cyclic voltammograms were collected during the adsorption process of blood lead at predetermined time, and the residual blood lead concentrations were also measured by ICP-MS simultaneously to calculate the adsorption capacity (Q, μg/g).

The gelation process of GB hydrogel is a fast and easy sol-gel transition driven by multistep hierarchical self-assembly process (Peters et al., 2015). As shown in Scheme 1 , Guanosine (G) and B(OH)3 can form guanosine-borate diesters (GB diesters), followed by the formation of GB quartets with sodium ions (Na+) as the cation template, which can further stabilize the structure. GB quartets stack one by one together through π-π stacking to form GB wires, the sides of which are further entangled with borate bonds to drive the gelation of GB hydrogel. In our recent study (Wu et al., 2020b), we have performed a series of characterization including FTIR, 1HNMR and XRD, which confirmed the existence of guanosine-borate diesters and G4-quartet structure in the GB hydrogel.

The obtained GB hydrogel has superior properties well-suited for hemoperfusion adsorbents, among which the stability under physiological environment and self-healing ability were characterized. The stability of the GB hydrogel was first confirmed in PBS or saline, this hydrogel could keep the weight basically constant up to 72 h (Fig. S1, Supplementary information) and the stable pH range was in the pH 4.5–10.5 (Fig. S2). This good stability enabled the hydrogel to remain stable in physiological media without dissolution and degradation, thereby reducing degradation product-induced side effects.

As the adsorbent material for blood perfusion, the hydrogel needs to undergo the shear force of blood flow. Self-healing properties endow hydrogel with the capacity to resist external forces, recovering its original shape and functionality from physical damage (Shi et al., 2019). The self-healing process of GB hydrogel was characterized by direct observation and rheological measurement (Fig. S3) and confirmed that GB hydrogel was able to withstand external shear forces and endowed with the ability to maintain integrity and stability in fluids. Therefore, GB hydrogel with stability and self-healing properties is a promising soft material for applications under a strong shear in fluids such as blood flow and other flowing environmental water.

Blood compatibility is essential for adsorbent material used in blood purification and were assessed through hemolytic property, RBCs morphology and coagulation assays (Figs. S4 & S5). It demonstrated that GB hydrogel did not cause significant hemolysis and morphology alteration of RBCs up to 24 h as well as not affect plasma coagulation, suggesting its blood-compatibility for the potential blood contacting applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GB hydrogel after lead ions adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the lead ions adsorption property of GB hydrogel, the GB hydrogel with and without lead ions treatment was characterized by various techniques. It was found that the lead ions treatment significantly hardened the hydrogel and turn into white on its surface in comparison to saline control (Fig. 1 a). This might be attributed to the higher charge density of lead ions that displayed stronger interaction with anionic borate esters compared with sodium ions (Plank et al., 2017), thereby the bound lead ions increased the crosslinking density of the hydrogel network and further reduced the transparency of the hydrogel. To prove this hypothesis, we observed the morphologic structure of GB hydrogel before and after lead ions treatment by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). SEM images showed that the pore size of the gels treated with lead ions was significantly decreased and thereby the gel became more compact (Fig. 1b). The statistics of the pore area distribution also supported the as-mentioned result. The SEM results indicated that the crosslinking density of GB hydrogel was enhanced after the adsorption of lead ions, indirectly supporting the effective adsorption of lead ions by GB hydrogel.

Rheological experiment of the hydrogel treated with a gradient concentrations of lead ions-containing saline was further conducted. Elastic modulus of the hydrogel measured in a single-frequency time sweep mode increased significantly with the increase of lead ions concentration (Fig. 1c). The positive correlation between elastic modulus and the lead ions concentration proved that the adsorption of lead ions can effectively increase the crosslinking density of the hydrogel, thereby improving the mechanical properties of the hydrogel. Circular dichroism (CD) spectra of GB hydrogel showed a positive peak at 217 nm, which was enhanced considerably after the adsorption of lead ions. The CD signals of GB hydrogel are the result of chiral nucleosides assembling into supramolecular chirality, rather than the inherent molecular chirality of individual nucleosides (Adhikari et al., 2014). The enhancement of the positive peak in CD spectra demonstrated that the adsorbed lead ions, as the cation template, improved the self-assembly of G4-quartet and enhanced the intertwining of the GB wires (Fig. 1d).

On the basis of results (visual observation, SEM, rheology and CD), lead ions can be effectively adsorbed by GB hydrogel through replacing the original sodium ions in G4-quartet and its higher charge density can increase the crosslinking density of the gel network, thereby enhancing the tightness and mechanical strength of the hydrogel as well as altering its assembly conformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lead ions adsorption property of GB hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption performance of GB hydrogel for lead ions in aqueous solutions was evaluated. Within the time-dependent adsorption model, as shown in Fig. 2 a, lead ions in aqueous solution were adsorbed rapidly at the beginning and the adsorption percentage reached 70% within 1 h. Then adsorption rate gradually slowed down when approaching the equilibrium and final adsorption equilibrium was reached at about 3 h when the adsorption percentage was about 84%. Kinetic model was used to estimate the possible mechanisms of the adsorption kinetics (Ho 1999). It can be seen from Fig. 2b that the fitting curve of pseudo second order model (PSOM) had a high correlation coefficient with the kinetic data (R2 = 0.9986), while the fitting curve of pseudo first order model (PFOM) had almost no correlation (Fig. S6a). The PSOM adsorption kinetics model mainly describes the surface adsorption through the exchange of electrons between the adsorbent and the adsorbate which take place in chemisorption (Robati 2013), whereas PFOM represents a diffusion-based theory. Therefore, the fitting result of PSOM indicated that the lead ions adsorption in this process was an adsorption involving chemical interaction between GB hydrogel and lead ions, rather than the simple lead ions diffusion.

Fig. 2c&d showed the effect of initial concentration of lead ions on adsorption capacity and the adsorption isotherms model used to describe the solid-liquid adsorption process. With GB hydrogel as the adsorbent in aqueous solution at 37 °C, the lead ions adsorption capacity increased linearly when the initial concentration of Pb2+ below 400 mg/L. While over 400 mg/L, the adsorption capacity increased slowly and gradually reached a plateau, eventually reaching the maximum at about 46 mg/g. Freundlich adsorption model was used to describe the adsorption isotherms.

Other than Langmuir isotherm that assumes the adsorption process is a monolayer adsorption onto a total homogeneous adsorbent surface, the Freundlich isotherm assumes that the surface of the adsorbent is a heterogeneous surface in which the active sites and their energies distribute exponentially (Chung et al., 2015; Saadi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2007). Compared with the Langmuir model (Fig. S6b), the Freundlich model provided a better fitting result (R2 = 0.9959), indicating that the Pb2+ adsorbed on hydrogel via a multilayer manner.

Other than adsorption model, we also evaluated the Pb2+ adsorption capacity of GB hydrogel in comparison to the commercially available activated charcoal (Fig. S7), it indicated that the adsorption capacity of the GB hydrogel is significantly higher (approximately 2 times) than the adsorbent activated charcoal. Moreover, further experiments showed that the hydrogel could keep the Pb2+ capacity in the storage stably over 48 h (Fig. S8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective adsorption ability and renewability of GB hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the non-selective adsorption property of conventional adsorbents (such as activated charcoal) to cause the loss of major components in blood (Venkataraman et al., 2003), using these adsorbents to contact with blood may further lead to thrombocytopenia and neutropenia (Pond 1991). For this reason, we compared the adsorption of plasma protein and RBCs in the GB hydrogel in comparison to activated charcoal.

As shown in Fig. 2e, with the adsorbent amount increased, the plasma protein adsorption in GB hydrogel remained negligible (<5%), while the adsorption percentage on activated charcoal increased significantly, reaching a maximum value of approximately 35% of the plasma protein at 5% (w/v) adsorbent/plasma ratio. Meanwhile, the adsorption behavior of the two adsorbents for RBCs showed similar trends (Fig. 2f), in which 5% (w/v) of activated charcoal caused the adsorption of 21% of RBCs, whereas the value of GB hydrogel was only 8%. These results demonstrate that GB hydrogel does not cause a significant adsorption of main blood components in comparison to activated charcoal, thus suitable for the application in long-time contacting with the blood without causing loss of RBCs or plasma proteins.

To prove that the lead ions adsorption by GB hydrogel will not be significantly interfered by the other ions in the blood, we evaluated the competitive adsorption of the different metal cations by the hydrogel (Fig. 2g). After incubating the hydrogel with the lead ions solution containing the same concentration of other three major metal cations (K+, Na+ and Ca2+) in blood, it was found that the removal efficiency of Pb2+ could reach more than 90%, while the adsorption of Ca2+ could reach 60%, and the adsorption of Na+ and K+ was only about 10%. The reason for different adsorption capacity of the hydrogel on different ions may be that the lead ions have a higher charge density and have a stronger electrostatic interaction with the anionic borate esters in the hydrogel network (Plank et al., 2017). The results suggested that the hydrogel could adsorb lead ions more efficiently in comparison to other metal cations in blood.

To demonstrate the renewability of GB hydrogel, EDTA-2Na, with high chelating ability toward Pb2+ was selected as the regenerant in the cyclical adsorption-regeneration experiment. In 3 times of adsorption-regeneration cycles (Fig. 2h), over 90% the adsorbed lead ions were effectively desorbed from the hydrogel in regeneration process. Moreover, the regenerated hydrogel was able to maintain almost the same adsorption capacity for another cycle of Pb2+ enrichment, indicating that GB hydrogel is a recyclable adsorbent with considerable renewability.

In Comparison to the adsorption of lead ions from aqueous solution, it is usually much more complicated to eliminate lead from blood. Therefore, we evaluated the potential of GB hydrogel as a blood lead adsorbent in time-dependent mode and cyclic adsorption mode, respectively. More importantly, we used human blood to prepare lead-poisoned blood, which simulated clinical application as far as possible. As shown in Fig. 3 a in the time-dependent mode with single adsorption module, GB hydrogel adsorbed more than 30% of the blood lead in 0.5 h, then the adsorbance gradually reached equilibrium. After 3 h, the adsorption of blood lead was saturated and the removal percentage was approximately 48%, resulting in the decrease of blood lead concentration from 800 to 416 μg/L (Fig. 3b). The clearance of blood lead by GB hydrogel was significantly higher than that of the activated charcoal control group (about 34%). In cyclic adsorption mode, a significant higher adsorption efficiency (more than 60%) was achieved at 4th cycle (Fig. 3c and d). It indicated that cyclic adsorption with multiple adsorption modules would further improve the clearance efficiency of blood lead. This hemopurification efficiency can be further improved by adjusting the hydrogel/blood ratio in each adsorption module, as well as the adsorption cycles and number of adsorption modules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative visualization and electrochemical quantification of lead ions adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Smart medical devices with qualitative visualization and quantitative signal monitoring have drawn more and more attention. The introduction of intelligent detection techniques into the treatment module of medical devices will synchronize therapy and diagnosis, providing brand new solution with integration and intelligence. Due to the unique structure, a blood lead selective adsorption system based on GB hydrogel integrated with real-time visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitoring was designed (Scheme 2 ). Specifically, lead poisoned blood would be poured through TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel in a column-like perfusion device, where the lead ions are selectively adsorbed without non-specific retention of blood components. Upon contacted with lead ions, the TMPyP bound in GB hydrogel will undergo visible color change, qualitatively presenting lead ions adsorption extent. Simultaneously, the device acts as an electrolytic cell, where the electrochemical signals of GB hydrogel as a working electrode are detected to quantitatively denote the adsorbed amount of lead ions, thus reflecting blood lead levels while receiving treatment.

Monitoring heavy metal ions by visual sense is efficient and intuitive (Kumar et al., 2017; Meng et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2020). Herein, we propose a strategy to combine GB hydrogel with TMPyP probe that can specifically sense lead ions by exhibiting a characteristic electron absorption band in the visible light region (Sallam et al., 2020), endowing it with the property for qualitative lead ions visualization. . According to previous reports, TMPyP can be intercalated into G4 quartets stacking in GB Wires (Cavallari et al., 2009; Lee et al., 2003). Moreover, the electrostatic interaction between the cationic TMPyP and anionic GB diester can further stabilize the TMPyP in the hydrogel. Release profiles of TMPyP bound in GB hydrogel showed that the cumulative leakage of TMPyP within 24 h was negligible (Fig. S9), suggesting that TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel would not bring the risk of secondary blood contamination.

To confirm the stable combination between TMPyP and the hydrogel, rheological and UV–Vis spectra experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the elastic and viscous modulus of GB hydrogel increased significantly after binding TMPyP (G′ from 5500 Pa to 11,000 Pa). Moreover, the UV–Vis spectra exhibited the absorption peak of TMPyP shifted from 420 nm to 435 nm after binding to the hydrogel (Fig. 4b). These results demonstrated that TMPyP effectively bounded into GB wires via intercalation, rather than being physically encapsulated in the hydrogel. We further verified TMPyP for the detection of lead ions and found that the UV–Vis Spectra of both TMPyP aqueous solution (Fig. S10) or TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel (Fig. S11) showed shift and rise of the absorption peak with the lead ion concentration increased, suggesting that the color of TMPyP was changed with the lead ions concentration. Besides, lead ions could cause a significant fluorescence quenching of TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 4c). It's worth noting that the color of the hydrogel changed from brown to green along with the increased concentration of lead ions (Fig. 4d). Therefore, benefiting from binding with TMPyP, GB hydrogel was endowed with the ability to qualitatively and rapidly diagnose the presence and approximate quantity of lead ions in hydrogel by visual observation during elimination treatment.

Other than qualitative visualization of lead ions by introducing TMPyP probe in GB hydrogel, we further utilized the intrinsic conductivity of GB hydrogel to quantitatively monitor the lead ions adsorption quantity in the hydrogel by electrochemical detection. The schematic diagram of the working electrode of the electrochemical monitoring device was shown in Fig. S12. Since the cations adsorbed in hydrogel could result in a change on the current flow through the hydrogel, the changes in the cyclic voltammograms of the hydrogel was measured during the lead ions adsorption process. As shown in Fig. 4e, the GB hydrogel without lead ions adsorption (black line) had the lowest anodic peak current at a potential of 0.4 V, while a significant increase in anodic peak current occurred during the adsorption process of lead ions. Moreover, there was a high linear correlation between the anodic peak current and the adsorption amount of lead ions (Fig. 4f). The above results demonstrate that GB hydrogel can be used as an electrochemical sensor for the real-time quantitative monitoring of lead ions adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the bi-modal monitoring system during blood lead adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of GB hydrogel-based bi-modal monitoring system in hemopurification application, we conducted the above-mentioned qualitative and quantitative monitoring experiments in the lead poisoned human blood. As shown in Fig. 4g, the color of TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel changed from brown to green after incubation with blood containing 800 μg/L of Pb2+. TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel/ITO, as the working electrode, showed the increase in anodic peak current along with blood lead adsorption, which was consistent with the results obtained in saline. Besides, the anodic peak current and the lead ions adsorption amount displayed a good linear correlation (Fig. 4h). It suggested that the adsorbed blood lead ions could be determined by visualized color change and quantitative current signals.

From the above results, it can be concluded that TMPyP-bound GB hydrogel is able to selectively remove blood lead synchronized with rapid perceiving the blood lead existence by simple visualized observation and accurate reflecting the adsorbed blood lead amount through electrochemical monitoring. Moreover, this bi-modal monitoring system also reminded the saturation adsorption needing regeneration.

In summary, we proposed a selective blood lead adsorbent integrated with real-time visualized/electrochemical bi-modal monitoring based on TMPyP-bound guanosine-borate supramolecular hydrogel as potential smart hemoperfusion device. The employed GB hydrogel possessed stability in physiological environment, self-healing ability resistant to fluid shear, blood compatibility, selective adsorption capacity of lead ions rather than blood components (RBCs, plasma, physiological metal ions) and renewability. The adsorption mechanism of lead ions in GB hydrogel was attributed to that the lead ions with higher charge density replaced the original sodium ions template in the G4-quartets, resulting in increased crosslinking density and mechanical strength of the hydrogel. Thus, the adsorption kinetics fitted to the chemisorption via a multilayer manner. Benefiting from binding TMPyP into the GB hydrogel, it was endowed with the ability to qualitatively and rapidly diagnose the presence and approximate quantity of lead ions in blood via simple color change. By virtue of the intrinsic conductivity, GB hydrogel was harnessed as an electrochemical sensor to accurately and quantitatively reflect the adsorbed lead amount from blood. Therefore, real-time bi-modal monitoring involving qualitative visualization and quantitative electrochemical sensing was synchronized with hemopurification treatment. This work provides insight into the integrated treatment/monitoring device with high selectivity, simple fabrication and low-cost, paving a way for the next generation design of intelligent, precise theranostic hemoperfusion system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81701808 and 31870943) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (No. 2021A1515011093), China.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2021.113230.

Xiang Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xin Yan: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Bin-cheng Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Visualization. Wei Xue: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision. Chang-ren Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology. Yi Zhang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zong-hua Liu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
